paragraphs.body: It was a Thursday night in November which meant that I had basketball practice from 8-9:15 at West Middle School which was a good twenty minutes away from my house. But like always we are going with our friends, with whom we have a great carpool system. Once we got picked up by our other friend's mom we all got ready to go see if our couch was in a good or bad mood today. While in the car we are listening to B101.1 which plays a mixture of Adele, Taylor Swift, and any other song. After about 30 minutes, we arrived at the middle school, got dropped off, and thanked Mrs. Silas for driving us to practice. The four of us walk into the building fast because it is very cold outside and we are all wearing our sweatpants over our shorts and Crocs/slippers because we do not want to damage the tread on our basketball sneakers from walking on the sidewalk. Once we reach the door we find our teammates at the end of the lobby/hallway where we see the other team that will use the other half of the court tonight getting ready for their own practice. When we reach our team neither of our coaches have arrived yet but we all know that we have to get our basketball shoes on and get ready to go practice. Then the dreadful hour of 8 rolled around and we already started our 3 mandatory laps before practice started. During our laps our couch is yelling “You cut corners here you cut corners in life.” He said this to remind us that if you cut the corners when running you will have to run another lap for every girl that he says cut the corner of the court during the laps. “Two roads diverged in a yellow wood, And sorry I could not travel both And be one traveler, long I stood And looked down one as far as I could To where it bent in the undergrowth;” (Robert Frost) Travel basketball all started when we were in third grade and ended when we got to eighth grade due to the idea that you would go and play high school ball. I grew up with those girls and we would spend countless hours practicing every week for years. Looking back, we all jokingly say that hearing this phrase years after basketball ended brings some uncomfortable feelings. But for me, I think now I can see the real meaning behind the phrase that sort of became a catchphrase of our coach after all those years. I believe that our coach was trying to tell us that in life you cannot always cut your way through life. I believe that the meaning behind the phrase was to teach us something about how the world works and how your actions might affect you and the people around you.

paragraphs.body: I have learned a very valuable lesson from this phrase that I heard constantly growing up and has resulted in me being able to grow as a person from it. If I never listened to this phrase I could never have understood the value of doing everything to your full extent or not know that my actions might also have consequences for other people. I know that throughout your educational years, you constantly hear about if you get caught what will happen to you but that is if you get caught the consequences could take time. While at practice the consequence occurred right after someone got caught we got punished. It taught me that in life you will always receive some sort of consequence if you get caught doing something deviant. It has taught me to always be honest and respectful in any situation. In the end, running the laps was never the worst thing about practices; they were just the things that could cause something else to happen. They were just the things that people would always try to take the shortcut to, which was never a good idea. They were the things that taught me the most about life and what happens when you try to shorthand life. They were the things that even though they killed me sometimes caused me to think about what was at stake for everyone. To tell the truth, they were the things that I still think about to this day because their meaning was not to build up stamina. Their meaning was to never try and take the shortcut in life or else something unpleasant might happen that you did not want to happen.

annotations.comment: So interesting. Yes--actions have consequences for _other_ people.

paragraphs.body: For years I would hear this phrase being said to us or screamed at us depending on what kind of mood our coach was in. When I was younger I never quite understood why he would constantly say it when we all knew what would happen if he caught someone. We all knew from a young age that something bad was going to happen if he caught you trying to cheat your way through the laps. We all knew that if one person did it then we all would be punished. We all knew the consequences of our actions and knew that it would affect everyone on the team. But why did some girls still decide to do it even though it would affect everyone else on the team? “Then took the other, as just as fair, And having perhaps the better claim, Because it was grassy and wanted wear; Though as for that the passing there Had worn them really about the same,”(Robert Frost) For years I have questioned why some of the girls decided to cut the corners even though they knew that it would ultimately lead to everyone having to run more laps. Why would they cut the corners on the laps that we always did knowing that we only had to run three? Why would they decide to add extra running on top of the already planned running for the start of practice? Why would they add extra work to be done at the beginning of practice when they knew we would run more later in practice? Why was it usually the same girls who decided to cut the corners each time it happened?

annotations.context_left: And having perhaps the better claim,

annotations.comment: Great use of questions! It shows your critical inquiry skills on the page. Neat.
annotations.context_right: Why would they add extra work to be done at the beginning of practice when they knew we

paragraphs.body: I think that it all comes down to how people were raised and what kind of parenting styles their parents decided to partake in when raising their kids. I think that some of the girls got handed whatever they wanted which caused them to think that they could just take shortcuts whenever they pleased. They thought that because outside of basketball they had the privilege of getting whatever they wanted it would also apply to them on the court. But that is not how life goes. The girls who were not raised like that were usually the girls who did not cut the corners. The girls who cut the corners were usually the girls who off the court got handed what they wanted. The girls who did not cut corners were raised by parents who taught them that not everything will come easy to you and that you are going to have to work for what you want. This is shown on the court at practice with running laps, the girls who were raised with the mindset that you have to work for everything even if you think that it is stupid rarely cut the corners. While the girls who were handed things still had the mindset of working hard and giving everything they went to what you do. But they thought that because the laps seemed stupid they could cut the corners. The difference between how each girl was raised showed how each girl took the idea of running the laps at the beginning of practice.

annotations.comment: Really interesting ideas in this paragraph! I wonder, though, if some of your sentences are redundant? Could you simplify this paragraph?
annotations.context_left: wanted. The girls who did not cut corners were raised by parents who taught them that not

paragraphs.body: In life, you might think that taking a shortcut will cause you to be happier and cause you to get something done faster but in reality it will only cause pain. The truth of the matter is that when you try and do something faster than it should be done it will most of the time not work. For basketball, it resulted in more laps and more running when getting caught. The phrase “cut corners here you cut corners in life” resulting in this realization that nothing in life is going to be easy and that you are always going to have to try your hardest. I have learned through watching people doing it that you can never just try and take the shortcut. If you try and bypass anything then you will always deal with some unpleasant consequences. “And both that morning equally lay In leaves no step had trodden black. Oh, I kept the first for another day! Yet knowing how way leads on to way, I doubted if I should ever come back.” (Robert Frost) In life, I have seen how differently some people were raised and it reflects in the way that they treat other people and other things around them. Working in retail has shown me this and has proven to me what I have always believed about how I was raised. I was raised to treat people kindly and to always respect authority no matter what. When working at the store I have seen people be completely oblivious about their surroundings and do not care that we close in five minutes. So they decide to take their good old time looking around. Some customers when trying clothes on in the fitting rooms tend to think that we are just their maids cleaning up their mess. I have seen people just leave piles and piles of half-inside-out clothes and hangers everywhere in the fitting room and just leave the room in a complete and utter mess. Those customers have a lack of awareness of the people working there when it comes to making sure that we can give each customer our best attention and make sure that the store stays in good shape.

annotations.text: Working in retail has shown me this and has proven to me what I have always believed about how I was raised. I was raised to treat people kindly and to always respect authority no matter what.
annotations.comment: Interesting connection! Nice job going to your own life experience :)

paragraphs.body: This lack of awareness is what I believe is developed within someone and formed depending on how they were raised and what their parents taught them. Compared to some of my friends I would say that my parents raised me the right way and in a way that I can see the world through a different lens compared to other people. My parents raised me to care and respect everyone around me and everything around me. My parents raised me to believe that you should value what everyone else around you has to say even if you do not fully believe them or agree with them. They taught me that in life not everything is going to come easy and that you will have to work for a lot of things in life. They taught me that life is not always going to be easy and life will always throw curve balls out at you but you always going to have to try to fit those battles. They taught me this by example and throughout life I have seen how they have battled the curve balls that life throws at them. I have seen how even if they are struggling they will always try to put their best foot forward for us and never let their battles affect us kids.

annotations.text: They taught me that in life not everything is going to come easy and that you will have to work for a lot of things in life. They taught me that life is not always going to be easy and life will always throw curve balls out at you but you always going to have to try to fit those battles. They taught me this by example and throughout life I have seen how they have battled the curve balls that life throws at them.
annotations.comment: Isn't it interesting that this is almost a definition paragraph? What a cool move to make--the repetitions are great :)

paragraphs.body: I believe that metaphorically like my coach did for all those years of basketball my parents also taught me to always touch the line. I think having heard this quote from such a young age and having it stick with me for years now has caused me to think about the different actions that I have partaken in. I think that it has caused me to always try my hardest and never try to cut corners in life. I think that it has caused me to think about what my actions might do and how they will affect other people. I also believe that what my parents taught me about respecting authority and caring about others has played a huge part in how I perceive the world. My parents taught me that you should always take care of yourself and the people you decide to keep close to you. They also taught me to never give up and never try to conform to anything. I believe that what my parents and my coach instilled in me from such a young age helped me have a better understanding of what can happen in the world. “I shall be telling this with a sigh Somewhere ages and ages hence: I took the one less traveled by, And that has made all the difference.” (Robert Frost)

annotations.comment: I'm interested in the metaphor you've created--taking the "long" route instead of the shortcut is the one less traveled by most people. So cool.

paragraphs.body: At last, the practice began and we started our three laps around the court with our coach telling us that “if you cut corners here you cut corners in life”. On this Thursday night in November, no girls cut the corner of the court which meant that we only had to do three laps to warm up for this practice. The phrase that we soon all learned to love/hate would stay with me for years now and has taught me that people grow up differently than I did and were taught different lessons than me. The phrase also taught me to never take the shortcut in life when doing something important in life because if caught consequences will occur. And just like that the practice was over and we all took our shoes off and put our other shoes on. Until next Tuesday when we will be back at the same time in the same gym doing the same thing as this practice. Hoping and praying that no girls will cut corners when running our warmup laps so we will not have to hear our coach scream “You cut corners here you cut corners in life”.

annotations.text: And just like that the practice was over and we all took our shoes off and put our other shoes on.
annotations.comment: Nice job returning to the narrative in your intro!
